grades how will you get a grade? if you do more than 6, you get extra credit (each hw is worth 10 points) but max score before exams is 70 the course objective: know enough nlp / ml from this class so you can impress on a job interview if you want: you can write a survey paper and implement a model and/or try to extend some result and submit it: emnlp / acl / naacl i can help with this 5 pages is enough for a 'short' paper (can also be for a workshop)

grades: to get a+, you need to do well on exams and hw total points example (lots of extra hw but poor on exams): score = (min(100, 70) + 5 + 5) = 80 = b- total points example (minimal hw but good on exams): score = (min(58, 70) + 20 + 20) = 98 = a if you get a total score  90 you get a- if you get a total score  100 you get a+ (you did well on exams and did the minimal hw) if you get a total score  93 you get a if you get a total score  82 you get b- if you get a score  78 you get c+ if you get a score  62 you get d- if you get a score < 60 you get f might rescale this is needed to get some bell curve depending on the distribution; but i'll only rescale if it can help people
after class (in person) will try for wednesday before class also will announce on ed i'll try to go about 1.5 hours 2 hours each week, not 2.5

what background / compute do i need? do i need to know lots of deep learning / ml for this class? probably not, but you need to know math + python (conda / numpy) or you can pick up python i'll try to add in things as we go so there a little explanation for different things or a reference you can use to fill in a knowledge gap for this class, one goal i have is to go over details of some famous (nlp) papers and not just textbook material i.e what is word2vec? i.e how does bert work when you apply it to different tasks? (let's actually look at the internals) gpt? colab would be great so you have gpu access (especially if you want to do a project), but i'll try to make the work in such a way that you don't need it hw is meant for you to understand the basics, not do heavy compute experiments

one-hot representation: review this more if you need it features f that are categorical can be expressed as one-hot vectors: if f takes on 4 values f is a vector of 1/0 values and exactly one of these values is 1 similarly, the response for logistic regression can be written as a one-hot vector: 0 can map to (1, 0) and 1 can map to (0, 1) one-hot representations are important since they allow us to represent a categorical feature or response as a succinct vector but they have a problem assume we have a feature f that has 4 categories and categories are very much related (say'math' = 0, 'physics' = 1, 'painting' = 2, 'writing' = 3) if we have two instances f (1) = 'math' and f (2) = 'physics' which are have one-hot representations (1, 0, 0, 0) and (0, 1, 0, 0) then ( f (1) )  f (2) = 0 but, this might not make sense: ideally the inner product captures some 'similarity' between the two vectors since math and physics are related

auc: a review what makes a model good at classification for 1/0 response? logistic regression gives us two types of probabilities, p ( y = 1) and p ( y = 0) imagine you have a imbalanced data, where 95% of the data is y = 0 and y = 1 is only 5% of the data accuracy is not good here, since trivial classifiers give good results a random classifier (predicting y = 1 with 5%) would give an accuracy of approximately  90% a deterministic classifier that always predicts 0 would give an accuracy of 95% too other metrics, like recall and precision came about to address this; an intuitive one you should know is auc

how do you use auc? say you have 3 positive samples and 987 negative samples you see 97 % auc; how do you interpret this? on average, across the 3 samples, this means that the score of one of the positives is larger than 97 % of the score given to true negatives so, this means that on average, for each positive samples, we expect to have 0.97 * 987 = 957 negatives we score higher than so, this means that there are roughly 30 negatives for each positive that have a higher probability score given by the model; the model is breaking down for these

top k sampling one direct way to remove the tail is topk sampling this is easy to explain: at each time step, consider only the k tokens with the highest probabilities when decoding at time t , select a set vtk of k top tokens set c =  xvtkp ( x | x 0 , , x t -1 ) renormalize and sample using q ( x | x 0 , , x t -1 ) = p ( x | x 0 , , x t -1 ) / c good: this method removes tokens with a low probability, so this is good because it truncates the tail problem: what is k ? should k always be the same? problem: to match human text, we need k large; this leads to incoherence problem: i.e you need low probability words sometime problem: q above varies greatly at each time step, hard to understand the gears
deeper dive into topk issues diagram

temperature scaling: fixing the unreliable tail fix t > 0 consider the token distribution p = softmax ( a ) but this time work with q = softmax ( a / t ) let t  0 as we saw, this has the effect of pushing tokens with high probabilities higher, and suppressing tokens with lower probabilities low temperature sampling can be used to partially alleviate the flatness / tail mass issues of topk sampling discussed above; by shaping the distribution before topk sampling problem: while lowering the temperature improves generation quality and eliminates the tail, it comes at the cost of decreasing diversity (see paper)

self-bleu and diversity given a set of hypotheses { g 1 , g 2 , , g t } , how do we measure diversity? for each generation, consider the bleu score when we look at g t vs the rest, with the rest serving as references we have: if the self-bleu is small, we have a large diversity (eg large k in topk , random sampling) if the self-bleu is large, we have a low diversity (eg beam search, low temperature, small k in topk ) upshot: nucleus sampling has self-bleu closest to human speech

gpt2 we saw encoder-decoder transformers can be used to get sota machine translation results by training to predict the next word, we also saw gpt-1 can be used generate text and do other tasks (via fine tuning) what happens if you make the model larger? what is 'inside' of gpt2 that we can use directly, without fine tuning? this was the first time 'prompt engineering' was used: we specify a context, and see what the model generates .. if we specify in the right way, we get better and better results mainly, this paper is about zero-shot learning no fine tuning, just a prompt trying to extract from the model some knowledge that is 'baked in'

the first large decoder how much larger was gpt2 vs gpt1? what are the differences? 100 million parameters  1.5 billion parameters small rearranging of layer norm and residual layers, another layer norm ln ( x + ff ( x ))  x + ff ( ln ( x )) mha ( x )  mha ( ln ( x )) increase vocabulary from 30 k to 50 k increase context window t from 512 to 1024 trained on webtext, consisting of crawls of 45 million links specifically, the authors show that a language model can learn general tasks without any fine tuning the authors believe that a large enough language model will begin to learn the tasks embedded within the natural language itself and won't require any additional supervision what are some examples?

albert: inference time vs bert albert-xxlarge has 200 million parameters bert-large has 340 million parameters however, albert-xxlarge has a larger structure, so inference is still slower authors discuss some other papers which could be used as a guide to speed it up big idea: it's not just all about more parameters!

vision transformer (vit) after bert came out, other variants like albert came out some dealt with text, but others dealt with other types of data as well we saw cnns for image classification and we used them to even do text classification can we take the transformer architectures we've seen for text and use them for image classification (i.e the reverse)? answer: yes

vision transformer at a high level, this is how a vision transformer works .. take the transformer encoder and use it to somehow ingest a picture and predict an image class but for a transform we had tokens, what are the tokens here? take a 224  224  3 picture break it up into patches of size 16  16  3 each of these patches are 'tokens' and we need them to be vectors so we map (using a cnn or mlp) each small volume to a vector for a transformer, we need to break ties using positional embeddings, so add these but, we need a prediction to make gradient updates and learn .. add a cls token at the start of the token sequence back propagate! given enough data, we get a very powerful classifier
vision transformer in pictures diagram

fine tuning given a transformer, what options do we have to use it for a specific task? let's focus on classification and bert, where we add a mlp on top of the cls token option1: we can optimize just the 'head' mlp parameters using bert as a feature extractor this is relatively cheap we do not, however, get sota on tasks option 2: we can optimize all of the parameters (bert + mlp) this is fine-tuning we can get sota but, the big problem is that this is quite expensive is there something in-between? we saw one example with ulm-fit: different learning rates were used per layer

lora both adapters and other methods like prefix-tuning (see references) have some issues adapters introduce high inference latency prefix-tuning is difficult to optimize, and performance changes non-monotonically in trainable parameters also, reserving a part of the sequence length for adaptation necessarily reduces the sequence length available to process a downstream task; might this lead to degradation in performance?
lora diagram

benefits of lora why would lora be a good idea? generalized full fine-tuning if we select r to to the right choice and have a lora set b , a for each matrix, we basically have fine tuning adapters converge to an mlp prefix models cannot handle longer sequences benefit of lora: no additional layers, so no additional inference latency another benefit: at test time, we can use w = w + ba , just a new matrix .. we always need to load 1 model yet another: we can swap in different ba for different tasks, so it's easy to 'adapt' the model for their paper, the authors focus on just the self attention parameters like w k or w q ; they freeze all the other parameters

recommendation problems recommendations! general problem: given a user's query, tell me the most relevant items amazon! walmart! given a search query, tell me the most relevant products document search given a set of documents and a list of sentences or chunks for each document, tell me the most relevant chunks and map those back to the source documents they came from given a search from a user, display relevant ads dispersed in within the search results page given a user lands on is home page, suggest some friends
recommendation problems diagram

recommendation problems: what do we need to do? given a problem, there are many things to consider what data do we have? what labels do we have? what models do we use? how do we know what metrics to look at? offline metrics? online metrics? what is the scale? is it serving fast enough? do recommendations need to be fresh upon request or can they be somewhat stale? what else?

metrics let's step back a second .. suppose we have a model such that, given some query, gives us the most relevant items ranked from best to worst how do we know that this is doing well? goal: have a metric such that if we do well we have a value of 1 if we do poorly we have a value of 0 'well' here means that the results returned are relevant to the user
offline metrics: recall diagram
offline metrics: recall @ k diagram

offline metrics: precision @ k we saw with recall that the denominator can become small .. we are making k predictions so ideally we have a metric which can be at most 1 and worst 0 .. recall @ k can be at worst 0 and at most k | i ( q ) | we have the definition here, we make k predictions at at most k of them are relevant .. so the fraction above is at most 1 and at worst 0 but we are not sure if we always can touch 1 precision @ k = number of relevant items among the top k predictions k

offline metrics: ap issues with alternative definition to do average precision right you really should have k be dependent on i ( q ) since the range of ap depends on i ( q ) for example, one query might have 5 relevant items and another might have 2 .. so, if you want to see how well you do on all queries you might want to consider k to be the maximum of i ( q ) for all queries specifically, with this k , for either of the above queries, the maximum ap is 1 and you really only get rewarded if you put things at the front for all queries

online metrics: ctr, cvr, cvs? all the metrics we've discussed above are offline metrics: we have a machine learning model and we want to get it to rank things as well as possible the analysis we do is done in a research / notebook setting, using historical data .. what happens once we deploy? in that setting, a user types a query and then gets list of results .. how do we tell if the results were good? how do we build a larger system that uses the ranking model we evaluated? let's first look at the basic metrics which we would use to evaluate system-level performance
online metrics: ctr, cvr, cvs? diagram

problem so back to the concrete problem: given a user and a textual search, we want to present the best videos to this user some assumptions there are 1 m videos there are 1 m users we have lots of information about interactions i.e when a video is watched we know its name, if it was clicked, finished or what percentage was watched, we know the age of the video, what channel it is displayed in, etc

problem: nail down what is going on usually, in situations like these you want to ask some questions to clarify .. for example question: is the input text-only, or can users search with an image or video? answer: text queries only question: does the platform serve more than videos? answer: just videos question: do i have information like video title and description? answer: yes; you have this and also the the video content question: do we have training data? answer: yes, let's assume we have about 10 m positive pairs (video id, text query)
problem: nail down what is going on diagram

strategy and speed considerations we have a problem .. for a given search and video (+ features), we need to say which videos would be the top choices (top 10, ranked) and we need to do this fast 'fast' means we need at a high level a quick way to map a query and some features to a top 10 list of videos
figuring out how to represent the query and the videos so given a query, we want to match videos which are similar to this query how can we represent a query how can we represent a video?

representing the query the query is text, and we know several ways to represent it we can use word2vec we take the embeddings for each token / word in the query and average over them we can use sentencebert we take the cls token and use it to represent the sentence or we average over all the other tokens in the sentence which one is better? word2vec very quick, but no semantic meaning .. words like 'bank' will always have the same embeddings and if a sentence is 'abcd' or 'dcab' we get the same representation via averaging bert better, contextual .. however, we can use variants of distilbert which are small and still give strong representations

representing the videos videos have a text part and a video part for the text part, we can use any of the methods we used for the query for the video part, there are a few options, but we want to summarize videos via an embedding one idea: consume the entire video, and create an embedding to summarize it this idea uses 3d convolutions and transformer models this is expensive, as we need to consume the entire video
representing the videos diagram

making sure the representations are close for the query and the text representations of the video, what do we know? well, we know that generally similar query text descriptions have high cosine similarity and low ones have a low similarity this was the idea of sentencebert and we saw that shallow models like word2vec also are ok to represent sentences what about the video embeddings vs the query search embedding?

making sure the representations are close given query text, we have a model that produces an embedding given some video frames, we have a model that produces embeddings the above two models are different, and we need to modify these models to yield embedding such that if the video and the query are the same, the inner product is large if the video and the query are different, the inner product is low what do we have and how can we use this?
making sure the representations are close diagram

how do we use these embeddings so now we have video embeddings which are close to query embeddings when appropriate .. we've placed video and text embeddings in the same space text embeddings which are naturally close / far now from similar and dissimilar embeddings

2 sentencebert models? what else? well, we need to be careful .. notice that we've changed sentencebert such that now a query embedding is close/far from a video embedding but we need to make sure that the 'old' properties of the sentencebert model still hold: similar queries have similar embeddings and dissimilar ones have low inner product to make things even easier, we can have a normalization layer above so that we don't work with inner product but cosine similarity .. the embeddings models produced are normalized

2 sentencebert models? for the sentencebert issue we can have a complex objective which still preserves the textual properties we started with for example, have the objective or data have query-video pairs and query-query pairs make another copy of sentencebert and fine tune just this one as above .. this way, in some sense, a query has a 'text' embedding and a 'text-video' embedding for this last idea, the query travels through both models
how do we use these embeddings diagram

how do we use the embeddings? crucially, note that for the videos we can have a service which computes text and video embedding and we can store these vectors them in a database at run time, we just compute the query's embedding(s) for a query, we get videos which are associated with the query-video text and and query-video content similarities for example, we can score videos using a combination of q  text v text and q  video v video use q  text v text to get a list and q  text v video to get a list and them take the union use q  text vtext + q  video vvideo 2 and just get 1 list once we have a set of videos, we can then re-rank them using a heavier model in the above, q video is what is coming out of the fine tuned sentencebert
so what might a full flow work like? which feeds the embeddings into a diagram

re-ranking? what else? in the last picture you see a ranking step the idea of this type of design is that we have a huge number of videos to select, and we first want to focus on retrieval and high recall so that we get a set of relevant videos for our query now, once we have a manageable set of size 100 (say), we can use a heavier model to refine our results with ranking .. at the ranking stage, we focus on precision as the key metric

anything before we rank? what else? the architecture we used was nice for a few reasons essentially, we used a biencoder for relevance this is a two-tower architecture which produces embeddings such that a search embedding is close to the video embedding this type of architecture is good because we can cache / save embedding and at runtime we just need the query embedding a set of videos might be huge and a standard search o ( n ) to find the 10 best, but there are methods to do this much quicker in o (log n ) time

candidate generator and knn at this step, we need to find, for a search, the closest videos here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding thus, we score videos via a score like q  v effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product the problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (mips) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user specifically, if there are | v | videos and each vector has dimensions d , finding the top k will be o ( | v | log kd ) (use a max heap) so, we'll need a way to trade off the accuracy of the nearest neighbors search in favor of a reduced latency .. we need to get rid of the | v |
lsh knn idea diagram

how does knn work? we have | v | videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be o ( | v | ) which is too expensive approximate knn algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to o (log | v | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval high level ideas? locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash .. at runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket tree based methods also work, and they partition the space of videos into regions .. at runtime, the query is projected to a region and similarly we just consider videos in that region for all the ideas above, the methods are tested and available to you, so you can use things like faiss or scann quite easily .. the error rates on these algorithms is quite low

re-ranking? what else? at this step, we can also use a crossencoder so that for each candidate video we get a score when we feed in the pair ( q , v ) where q is the query v is the video .. we do this for all videos we need to consider for example, if we have 100 videos we might break up the 100 into batches of size 10 feed these batches to servers which host the crossencoders and then get 100 scores and rank the videos based on these scores here, we batch since we want to distribute the load but this might not be necessary with the right model + gpu i.e if the model large, we might need this .. if it is small, we might not
re-ranking with a crossencoder and more diagram

re-ranking? what else? for each video, we also know features such as: historical ctr ema for 3, 5, 7 days, ema of likes for 3, 5, 7 days, differences between emas, age, topic, etc this is another idea but notice that we have a lot of features here but many fewer videos there are other cases: we can have multiple rakers where the rankers get more and more 'heavy' (i.e first we have a logistic regression, then an mlp / crossencoder hybrid but this last ranker runs on very few videos) after ranking, we can even have another phase that boosts new videos up, ensures diversity, etc these could be heuristic rules this last step is crucial also!

what if we wanted personalization? in this case, for two users who search the same query, we expect that the answers might not be the same .. we want to return answers that depend on the user id in this case, then, we need to build for each user a feature vector so that we can incorporate information into the model's ranking one idea: we have a system such that for a search query we return a set of relevant videos .. use our previous system to retrieve a set of relevant videos regardless of the user at the ranking step, we can impute user features such as age, location, sex, historical ctr, historical logins etc and use these features to rank videos but, ideally, we don't just do this at ranking time .. ideally we have a set of user embeddings per user and we use this at retrieval time

what if we wanted personalization? more topics for next time .. we'll need a way to figure out user embeddings what about new users? this is the cold-start problem for our previous analysis, we could have new videos but we just need to push these through the cnn and save the embeddings moreover, if we are not sure, we can use some sort of heuristic . for example: find the topic of the video use the average video embedding across videos in this topic what about bias? what if the model is large? what about click-bait videos? what is sparse search? i.e how else can we search beyond just using embeddings and why should we even bother?

getting a personal assistant is sft enough? using sft there are a few key limitations: data reflects human outputs, not preferences you're training the model to mimic what a human might say, but not to choose among many plausible options based on what humans prefer what about ambiguity and multiple good answers often, there's more than one good answer to a prompt sft only shows one per example, which may bias the model away from equally good or better answers sft doesn't capture what people like vs what's merely correct just because something is factually right doesn't mean it's helpful, kind, clear, or engaging sft doesn't optimize for human satisfaction or alignment with human values

the above model is called direct preference optimization at a high level, this is a reinforcement learning alternative which has no modeled reward function this encourages the model to assign higher probability to preferred outputs no explicit reward function is needed, but the loss function implicitly reflects preference dpo uses human preference data directly to optimize a contrastive loss between a preferred output and a less preferred one .. you could say the log-probabilities of preferred vs dispreferred outputs act as a proxy reward dpo is effectively matching the behavior of an implicit reward function that ranks outputs based on preference

dpo issues? issues with dpo? assumption mismatch . dpo is derived from a probabilistic model assuming humans make decisions based on a softmax over reward values but real human preferences are often noisy and inconsistent if the assumptions don't hold, the model can misinterpret the preference data sensitivity to preference quality .. dpo uses pairwise comparisons (e.g., a > b) to guide training if those preferences are sparse, inconsistent, or biased, the optimization can go astray there's no reinforcement loop to 'pull back' the model like in ppo (used in rlhf) less control over output distribution .. because dpo skips reinforcement-style feedback loops, it may offer less control over certain safety and behavioral constraints that can be more finely tuned with rlhf (e.g., toxicity reduction via explicit reward shaping) lack of exploration .. in rlhf (especially with ppo), the agent is encouraged to explore and learn from feedback dpo, being a supervised-style method, does not explicitly explore alternative outputs-it just learns from the comparisons you give it

why use rlhf? benefits of rlhf? explicit reward modeling .. rlhf gives you a separate reward model, which can be valuable for: auditing what the model 'likes' reusing it across models improving interpretability fine-grained control via reward tuning .. want your model to be funny and safe and informative? rlhf lets you weight these reward components explicitly and dynamically adjust the mix .. there is no 'optimal' implicit reward better performance in complex or high-stakes domains .. in cases where getting nuanced behavior is critical (like legal or medical advice), rlhf might better capture and enforce complex preference structures (+ rewards) proven track record .. many high-profile models (like chatgpt and claude) use rlhf because it's a mature and battle-tested method if you're aiming for production-level robustness, rlhf is still the gold standard
is there anything other than dpo? alternatives to dpo there are a few dpo alternatives .. ask chat gpt for some papers!

large batch sizes key benefits of large batch sizes computational efficiency: parallelism: larger batches allow for better utilization of hardware resources, especially gpus which excel at parallel matrix multiplication and other operations reduced communication overhead: your entire data is on the cpu and as you optimize you move it to the gpu (see above) the overhead of transferring data between the cpu and gpu is relatively fixed with larger batches, the data transfer cost per example becomes lower enhanced convergence: large batch sizes allow for better utilization of parallel processing hardware (gpus/tpus), reducing the time taken per epoch

large batch sizes key benefits of large batch sizes improved generalization? noisier gradient estimates: smaller batches can introduce more noise into the gradient estimates and generalize better (see later) ; batch norm and learning rate warming can help mimic this even with large batch sizes reduced variance: larger batches can reduce the variance of the gradient estimates; this can lead to smoother and more consistent updates to the model parameters larger steps: larger batches allow for taking larger steps in the optimization process this can accelerate convergence, especially in the early stages of training one idea: start with a large batch size and then decrease it (llama 3.1 used this)

gradient accumulation ok, so i want to use a large batch but i'm on colab and i have 1 gpu one idea: gradient accumulation .. process k batches each of size b sequentially for each batch, compute the loss gradient but do not do an update step instead, accumulate the k gradients by computing the gradient for a batch and sum with the previously computed gradient once you've processed k batches, then do a gradient step idea is now the gradient step is as if we've used a batch of size b  k
gradient accumulation diagram

what if you have multiple gpus? what would i do if i had 4 gpus and each could fit the model and a batch of maximum size 32? the idea now is something called distributed data parallelism idea: make a copy of each model, one on each gpu for a batch of size 128, split it into 4 chunks of size 32, and feed a batch of size 32 to each gpu compute the gradient of the model on each gpu note the model is assumed to be the same on each gpu once we have the 4 computed gradients, accumulate them as before to get a gradient which effectively is of size 128 put this gradient onto each gpu apply the same gradient step to each model on each gpu now, as before, each gpu has the same model but we've done a gradient step of size 128 effectively and not 32 ... distributed data parallelism is a library in pytorch you can leveredge

decoding: example once we train a model, we have the problem of decoding: how do we generate text using the model? usually, we start with a context like 'the man walks' or a special start token ' start ' and begin decoding with the model one very simple variant: greedy decoding this is one of the oldest and easiest heuristics

decoding: greedy search example basic idea: always pick the highest probability next word and use that as your next word once picked, add the word to your context and repeat until some terminal condition happens big idea is that, for example, we have p ( the , man , walks , y ) = p ( y | the , man , walks ) p ( the , man , walks ) we can just focus on p ( y | the , man , walks ) if we want to maximize p ( the , man , walks , y )

decoding: greedy search example so suppose the context is ' the man walks ' get argmax y p ( y | the , man , walks ) and suppose this is ' down ' note that technically we don't work with strings, but the integers that each string represent now the context is 'the man walks down' get argmax y p ( y | the , man , walks , down ) and suppose this is ' the ' now the context is ' the ' etc repeat this until the context is done or until a special end ' end ' token is generated once picked, add the word to your context and repeat over and over

decoding: beam search instead of picking the next highest probability word to add to your context, store a beam of size k as your set of candidates example: assume k = 2 and your context is again ' the man walks ' when we look at argmax y p ( y | the , man , walks ) p ( the , man , walks ) we don't just pick y = ' down ', we also pick ' fast ', which has the second highest probability now you have two context, ' the man walks down ' and ' the man walks fast ' next time, consider p ( y | the , man , walks , down ) p ( the , man , walks , down ) and p ( y | the , man , walks , fast ) p ( the , man , walks , fast ) and get the k = 2 maximum probability tokens

nmt: layers to get the best performance, we can use a (bidirectional) stacked lstm for the encoder the decoder rnn is autoregressive: it can't look into the future (it's a sort of language model!) we can use a stacked (one directional) lstm for the decoder the idea is that lower layers compute general features, while higher layers compute more complex interactions: both are crucial seems like 2-4 layers is about the sweet spot in the number of layers needed 2 layers better than 1 3 layers a little better than 2 the more layers you have, the more you'll need connections, tricks, etc

advantages compared to smt, nmt has many advantages better performance more fluent better use of context better use of phrase similarities 1 neural network, not 12 models in a disjoint 'pipeline' much less human engineering effort

how do we say a translation is 'good'? how do we measure the quality of a translation? source: le chat est sur le tapis mt system output: the the the the the the the reference 1: the cat is on the mat reference 2: there is a cat on the mat how do we know this is a bad translation?

one idea is to measure the precision of the translation for a general mt output we can get the precision by getting all the words (tokens) in the generated translation counting if each word is or is not in the reference translations dividing by the number of words in the generated translation for the example above we have 1 word 7 times 'the' is seen in at least one of the reference sentences reminder: precision = | hits | | predictions | for us, we get precision = 7 7 but this is bad, we clearly have a bad translation
bleu diagram

specifically, this is for each unique word w in the generated translation get count ( w ) , the number of times the word w is in the generated output get count clip ( w ), the minimum of count ( w ) and the maximum number of times w is in some reference sentence return w   y countclip ( w )  w   y count ( w ) with this definition, we get a precision of 2 / 7 since 'the' is in the generated translation 7 times and in the first reference 2 times

bleu so, clipping solved one problem but, what if we have translation 1: the cat is on the mat translation 2 (yoda): cat on the mat it is clearly, translation 1 is better but we would not pick this up if we just use tokens w that are unigrams (words) to this end, we can use bigrams, trigrams, and 4-grams to capture word order for example, for translation 1, we have bigrams 'the cat', 'cat is', 'is on', 'on the', 'the mat' we can then do the same exact thing as before, but this time use w as a bigram instead
the ranges of bleu indicate empirically how good the translation is diagram

more on positives and negatives positives it is quick to calculate and corresponds with the way a human would evaluate the same text it is used very widely, which makes it easier to compare your results with other work importantly, it is language-independent making it straightforward to apply to your nlp models it can be used when you have more than one ground truth sentence negatives it does not consider the meaning of words it is perfectly acceptable to a human to use a different word with the same meaning eg use 'watchman' instead of 'guard' but bleu score considers that an incorrect word it looks only for exact word matches sometimes a variant of the same word can be used eg 'rain' and 'raining', but bleu score counts that as an error it ignores the importance of words with bleu score an incorrect word like 'to' or 'an' that is less relevant to the sentence is penalized just as heavily as a word that contributes significantly to the meaning of the sentence it does not consider the order of words eg the sentence 'the guard arrived late because of the rain' and 'the rain arrived late because of the guard' would get the same (unigram) bleu score even though the latter is quite different

what is wordpiece? the authors used wordpiece which is a tokenization that expressed words like 'immaculate' as 2 tokens 'imma' + '##culate' 'immaculate'  'imma' + '##culate' the tokes you choose have an impact on your model .. what is wrong with just using words and characters? characters will almost surely cover all words (no '[unk]') but characters lose semantic meaning also, 4 words could map into 20 characters so then you have to keep a long context and have models which are good at memory on the other hand, words require huge vocabularies and also what do you do when you have a new word? instead of the most granular (characters) or the widest (words), find a better middle ground

what is wordpiece? high level idea of wordpiece: step 1: begin with a set of character tokens step 2: break up all words into tokens and get scores for tokens being next to each other step 3: from the most popular tokens next to each other, make a new token which is the concatenation of the above step 4: given the new token, get a count as in step 2 by greedily breaking up words into tokens step 5: repeat step 2 step 4 until you get a token universe of the desired size or you can't go on the transformer used 32000 tokens as its vocabulary v

word-piece: decoding with wordpiece, a word gets tokenized from left to right greedily; we try and use the largest token in the vocabulary as we go from left to right if we get to a place where a subword is not in the vocabulary, we classify the whole word as '[unk]' for example 'bugs' ['b', wp('##ugs')] ['b', '##u', wp('##gs')] ['b', '##u', '##gs'] 'hug' ['hu', wp('##g')] ['hu', '##g'] 'bum' ['b', wp('##um')]] ['unk'], since '##um' is not in the vocabulary

word embeddings motivation given : a corpus c with sentences want : a vector representation for each word a w such that the vector a w captures some of the 'meaning' of w for each w ideally : these vectors have nice mathematical properties if two words w 1 , w 2 are related, their vectors are related in the sense that a  w 1 a w 2 is high or, || a w 1 -a w 2 || is low: we can cluster similar words! maybe a w 1 -a w 2 has a 'meaning'; a doctor -a man  'medical profession person'

why word embeddings: motivation but why? .. there are a few textbook answers: traditional approaches treated words as independent entities, ignoring their meanings and relationships word embeddings, however, represent words as dense vectors in a continuous space, capturing semantic similarities (e.g., 'king' and 'queen' are close in the embedding space) one-hot encoding creates sparse, high-dimensional vectors (with a dimension for every unique word), which leads to inefficiencies in computation and storage pretrained embeddings can be used across various nlp applications, reducing the need for large labeled datasets tasks like sentiment analysis, machine translation, and named entity recognition benefit from embeddings, as they provide richer word representations than simple frequency-based methods models like word2vec utilize context to learn word meanings this helps in better distinguishing between usage (e.g.,'river bank' vs 'money bank') for word2vec, we actually always have 1 embedding for a word, so averaging the two embeddings above is crucial

hierarchical softmax : another trick to bypass o ( |v| ) hierarchical softmax is an efficient way of computing softmax the model uses a binary tree to represent all words in the vocabulary the v words must be leaf units of the tree for each leaf unit, there exists a unique path from the root to the unit; and this path is used to estimate the probability of the word represented by the leaf unit main advantage: we replace the o ( |v| ) softmax cost with o (log |v| ) the speed of this method depends on how the binary tree is built and words are associated with the leaf nodes; the paper uses a binary huffman tree good project? this layer is not available in pytorch by default
